evidence: All public tools use consistent (session, opts) two-parameter shape — connect/disconnect use throw semantics (documented in JSDoc: 'Unlike other toolkit tools which return ToolResult')

reply() validates input outside withToolWrapper, returns err with { path: 'local', latencyMs: 0 } instead of going through the wrapper — session.touch() not called, latencyMs is always 0 on validation failure
scan() opts parameter is optional (scan(session, opts?) signature), while attest/tip/verify/publish all require opts — inconsistency at optional vs required boundary
discoverSources() also uses optional opts parameter — consistent with scan() at least
All tools that pass through withToolWrapper use defaultErrorCode as a named positional param — the ordering (session, toolName, defaultErrorCode, fn) is consistent
connect() throws DemosError while all other tools return ToolResult<T> — intentional and documented
ScanOptions.filters is typed as Record<string, unknown> but is never read by scan() — accepted parameter with no effect
AttestOptions.claimType is typed and accepted but never used in attest() body — dead parameter accepted silently
No function has >5 parameters at the public API level — all use option objects or single opts
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: reply() bypasses withToolWrapper for validation, producing latencyMs=0 and skipping session.touch(). Two accepted options fields (filters, claimType) have no effect and create a false API contract.

evidence: attest(), pay(), and publish() all run SSRF validation (validateUrl) before making outbound requests — consistently protected

scan() calls bridge.apiCall('/api/feed') with no SSRF validation — but apiCall only accepts relative paths (enforced at bridge level: 'apiCall only accepts relative paths'), so the absolute URL SSRF vector is closed
verify() calls bridge.apiCall('/api/feed?limit=50') — same relative-path enforcement applies, not an SSRF issue
react() calls bridge.apiCall('/api/react') — same pattern, covered by bridge enforcement
tip() falls back to bridge.apiCall('/api/feed?limit=50') for recipient resolution — relative path, bridge-enforced

However, scan/verify/tip/react do NOT validate the content of API responses against injection (no schema validation on inbound data) while attest/pay/publish do validate inbound 402 response body (D402RequirementSchema)
Auth token (Bearer) is conditionally attached in apiCall — omitted when AUTH_PENDING_TOKEN, protecting against accidental token leakage
No permission literals as magic strings — auth patterns use explicit condition checks
Payee approval (requirePayeeApproval: true default) is consistently enforced in pay() — prevents untrusted payee bypass
getDemos() raw SDK access is guarded by allowRawSdk option — intentional bypass with explicit opt-in documented
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: scan/verify/react/tip call bridge.apiCall without per-tool SSRF validation — the bridge's relative-path check provides partial protection but does not validate DNS rebinding or HTTPS for the underlying apiBaseUrl set at session creation time.

evidence: tool-wrapper.ts catch block uses isDemosError() (imported from types.ts) inline — this IS the same function exported as isDemosErrorLike() from the same file, creating two near-identical type guards in the same module
safeReadBody in pay.ts imports safeParse via the guards/state-helpers module at the top (static import, line 15) — the prior open issue 'dynamic import' appears to be resolved
Comments in session.ts, connect.ts, sdk-bridge.ts explain WHY (TOCTOU, Symbol limitations, DAHR trust boundary) — not restating code
No nosy entry/exit logging across tool functions — only targeted console.warn for genuine degradation cases (auth fallback, tip recipient fallback)
sdk-bridge.ts extractTxHash() has a comment chain explaining the SDK version differences — legitimate, not restating
state-store.ts lock() uses inline lazy import for openFile — minor: imports 'node:fs/promises' at top already, so the inline import is redundant with the module-level import
tool-wrapper.ts exports both isDemosError (re-exported) and isDemosErrorLike which check the same shape — isDemosErrorLike is exported but isDemosError from types.ts is used for the actual check
pay.ts has a very long function body (240+ lines) but each step is labeled and logically sequential — not AI bloat, genuine protocol complexity

Defensive checks in transferDem (!to, !Number.isFinite(amount)) are on values that SHOULD be typed — these are internal bridge inputs that could arrive from an untyped caller path, arguably appropriate at bridge boundary
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: isDemosError and isDemosErrorLike coexist in tool-wrapper.ts — functionally identical type guards in the same module, one of which is unused from the module's own perspective.

evidence: Two write-rate-limit implementations actively coexist: src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts (sync/file-based, marked @deprecated at line 1) and src/toolkit/guards/write-rate-limit.ts (async/StateStore-backed)
cli/session-runner.ts line 80 imports from src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts (legacy) — active production code still using the deprecated version
cli/event-runner.ts line 30 also imports from src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts — both main CLI runners use the old system
index.ts lines 79-93: @deprecated comment on guard exports — 'will be removed in next major version' — but no timeline or version number specified

Deprecated exports include checkWriteRateLimit, recordWrite (write-rate-limit), checkTipSpendCap, recordTip (tip-spend-cap), checkPaySpendCap, recordPayment (pay-spend-cap), checkDedup, recordPublish (dedup-guard) — 8 deprecated functions in public surface
src/adapter-specs.ts lives at src/ root while src/adapters/ subdirectory exists — file placement inconsistency
No mixed JS/TS files found — full TypeScript
No stale migration TODO markers found with 'migrate', 'legacy', 'remove after' patterns in toolkit source
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Both CLI runners (session-runner.ts and event-runner.ts) actively import the deprecated legacy write-rate-limit implementation instead of the toolkit version. This is not an abandoned migration — it's a live split where the new system exists but the old system is still in production use.

evidence: src/lib/ has 42 files in a flat layout — contains auth, scoring, signals, state, sdk, feed-filter, llm-provider, claim-extraction, attestation-planner, quality-score, predictions, spending-policy, transcript, write-rate-limit, tips, tlsn-playwright-bridge, source-scanner, source-discovery, test-quality-validator, and more
src/lib/ contains a sources/ subdirectory (9 files) — mixed model where some domain grouping exists but most files are flat
src/toolkit/ is well-organized: types.ts, session.ts, state-store.ts, schemas.ts, sdk-bridge.ts, url-validator.ts, index.ts at root; tools/ (10 files), guards/ (6 files) in subfolders — good cohesion
src/adapter-specs.ts lives at src/ root despite src/adapters/ directory containing eliza/ and skill-dojo/ subdirectories — low fan-in straggler
cli/ directory contains 15+ CLI entry points — all entry points, not mixed concerns, flat is appropriate here
src/plugins/ is flat with 15 files — plugin-per-file is the convention, reasonable for this domain
src/reactive/ contains event-handlers/ and event-sources/ subdirectories plus 4 files at root — reasonably organized
src/adapters/ has two well-defined subdirectories (eliza/, skill-dojo/) — good

The src/lib/ flat overload is the primary issue: files like test-quality-validator.ts, write-rate-limit.ts, llm-claim-config.ts, skill-dojo-client.ts, skill-dojo-proof.ts, storage-client.ts, tlsn-playwright-bridge.ts, improvement-utils.ts span wildly different concerns
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: src/lib/ has 42 flat files mixing attestation, scoring, feed, auth, LLM, storage, state, and testing concerns. src/adapter-specs.ts is misplaced at src/ root.

evidence: state-store.ts: getDefaultStateDir() is a function (not a module-level constant) — homedir() is called at constructor time when FileStateStore is instantiated, not at import time. The previously flagged DEFAULT_STATE_DIR constant appears to no longer exist; getDefaultStateDir() is a function evaluated lazily.
sdk-bridge.ts: cachedTxModule and cachedD402Client are closure-scoped within createSdkBridge(), not module-level globals — correct pattern

discover-sources.ts: catalogCache is a module-level Map — shared across all sessions, but this is intentional (process-level cache, cleared via clearCatalogCache()). Static catalog data, not mutable state.
BUNDLED_CATALOG_PATH in discover-sources.ts uses import.meta.url at module load time — import.meta.url is a static ES module property, not a side effect
connect.ts: _isContainer is a module-level cached boolean — set once per process after first detectContainer() call. Benign: container detection is invariant and cached intentionally
session.ts: no module-level state — all state is per-instance
tool-wrapper.ts: no module-level state
DAHR_ERROR_KEYWORDS in sdk-bridge.ts is a module-level const array — frozen literal, no side effect
HIVE_PREFIX in sdk-bridge.ts is a module-level const Uint8Array — static literal, no side effect
No environment variable reads at import time in toolkit source files
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: The pre-existing open issue about DEFAULT_STATE_DIR appears resolved (now a lazy function). The _isContainer module-level singleton in connect.ts is minor but real initialization-coupling.

evidence: All toolkit tools return Promise<ToolResult<T>> consistently — return type annotations match all code paths including error paths (err() always returns ToolResult<T>)
connect() is documented as throwing DemosError rather than returning ToolResult — explicitly stated in JSDoc, not a hidden contract violation
disconnect() returns void — matches the implementation (session.expire())
session.touch() has no return type annotation on a void method — minor, not exported in public type

checkAndRecordWrite(store, address, record=false) — the record parameter default is documented in JSDoc as 'check only'; the dual-mode function (check vs. record) is a design choice but not a contract lie

getWriteRateRemaining() reads loadState without pruning — returns dailyRemaining based on state.entries.filter(e => e.timestamp > dayAgo), not the pruned state. Could return stale count if state hasn't been pruned recently (no lock acquisition). Minor discrepancy: the pruned count in checkAndAppend vs. the unpruned read in getWriteRateRemaining.
clearCatalogCache() — exported function that clears the module-level cache, name matches behavior
isDemosError() exported from types.ts — returns boolean, name matches
validateInput() returns DemosError | null — return type annotation matches all paths
SdkBridge.queryTransaction is declared optional (?) in interface — implementation in sdk-bridge.ts always provides it, but consumers must check for undefined per contract. Correct.
attestDahr(url, method='GET') — default method documented in signature, consistent with usage
contract_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: getWriteRateRemaining() reads unpruned state without a lock — can return optimistically stale counts compared to the locked pruned read in checkAndAppend.

evidence: verify.ts: [0, ...RETRY_DELAYS_MS] pattern correctly produces one immediate attempt + 3 delayed — clear and correct
verify.ts: if (delay > 0) await sleep(delay) is correct — skips sleep on first iteration (delay=0)
verify.ts: after all attempts, if lastError returns the CONFIRM_TIMEOUT error, else returns { confirmed: false } — the 'not found but no error' path correctly returns ok with confirmed:false
scan.ts: fetchFeed uses async/await correctly — bridge.apiCall is awaited. identifyOpportunities is sync (no await needed). No 'async without await' issue in the current code.
publish.ts: guards run in parallel with Promise.all then checked serially — efficient and clear
pay.ts: 'while (true)' loop in fetchWithValidatedRedirects with a MAX_REDIRECT_HOPS break condition — not truly infinite but the pattern is acceptable since the condition is checked first
pay.ts: Step 2 returns ok without receipt when non-402 response — correct early return, no dead code after it
state-helpers.ts: checkAndAppend always persists pruned state even on check-only paths (no entry) — intentional to prevent unbounded growth, comment explains why

discover-sources.ts: excludedStatuses is a Set<string> but the type comparison is 'has(s.status)' where status is SourceStatus (a string union) — functionally correct but Set<string> vs Set<SourceStatus> is a minor type precision issue

pay-spend-cap.ts: reservePaySpend calls unlock() before returning the success object (line 132) — the lock is released but the rollback function acquires a new lock when called. This is correct but non-obvious: the 'reservation' is held by the persisted entry, not an open lock.
sdk-bridge.ts: extractTxHash checks confirmResult ?? broadcastResult — ?? is correct (null/undefined coalescing)
logic_clarity.issues_preventing_higher_score: No critical logic errors found. Minor clarity issues: while(true) in redirect loop without a labeled exit, and verify.ts returns ok({confirmed:false}) after exhausted attempts which could be confused with a successful confirmation.

evidence: types.ts: all public types are explicitly typed with no any — DemosError, ToolResult<T>, StateStore, ConnectOptions all have typed fields
schemas.ts: bidirectional compile-time type assertions (lines 182-203) ensure schema output matches interface — forward and reverse checks, no drift possible
sdk-bridge.ts: DemosRpcMethods, D402ClientLike, TxModule are typed interfaces replacing bare 'unknown' or 'any' casts — single cast at factory level (line 182) is documented
sdk-bridge.ts: multiple Record<string, unknown> and unknown casts in extractTxHash and attestDahr response parsing — unavoidable due to SDK returning opaque shapes, but heavy casting density

discover-sources.ts: entry typed as Record<string, unknown> in catalog parsing, then cast with 'as string' on each field access — 8 type assertion casts in loadCatalog(). No Zod schema for catalog entries.
discover-sources.ts: excludedStatuses is Set<string> where SourceStatus would be more precise
connect.ts: WalletData interface has signingHandle: unknown — carries opaque parsed JSON
session.ts: (this as Record<symbol, unknown>)[AUTH_TOKEN] — necessary pattern for Symbol-keyed properties, TypeScript limitation
tool-wrapper.ts: isDemosErrorLike type guard uses 'code' in error && 'message' in error && 'retryable' in error — returns value typed as ReturnType<typeof demosError> which is DemosError. Fine.
ScanOptions.filters: Record<string, unknown> where filtering is not implemented — typed but dead
pay.ts: fetchWithValidatedRedirects return type is implicit Promise<Response> — not annotated
url-validator.ts: all functions have explicit return types
state-helpers.ts: generic constraints are properly typed (TState extends { entries: TEntry[] })
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: discover-sources.ts catalog parsing uses Record<string, unknown> with 8 cast expressions instead of a Zod schema or typed interface for catalog entries. sdk-bridge.ts has high cast density due to opaque SDK response shapes.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit has a well-designed consistent API shape: (session, opts) -> ToolResult<T> for all tools, with connect/disconnect as documented lifecycle exceptions using throw semantics. The main coherence gap is reply() performing validation outside withToolWrapper, producing a structurally valid but semantically different result (latencyMs=0, no touch). Additionally, two public option fields (ScanOptions.filters and AttestOptions.claimType) are accepted but never consumed, creating a false API contract.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The surface is largely consistent and the documented exception (connect throws) is explicitly called out. The reply() validation gap is a confirmed pre-existing open issue. Two dead option fields are a minor but real honesty problem. Score reflects a well-designed API with two narrowly scoped holes keeping it from the 85+ range.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: The chain-operating tools (attest, pay, publish) have thorough SSRF + allowlist + HTTPS enforcement consistently applied. The API-calling tools (scan, verify, react, tip) rely on the bridge's relative-path enforcement for SSRF protection, which closes the absolute-URL vector but does not apply IP-level DNS resolution checks. This creates a trust asymmetry between the two tool families — chain tools validate URLs at the IP level, API tools trust the bridge to whitelist the base URL.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The bridge's relative-path enforcement is a meaningful and intentional security boundary (not a gap), but it does not provide the same depth of protection as SSRF DNS validation. The pre-existing flagged issue about scan/verify/react/tip lacking SSRF is partially resolved by the bridge design but the per-tool depth of validation is lower. Score reflects a reasonably consistent authorization pattern with one identifiable asymmetry.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase is largely clean of AI-hallmark patterns: comments explain non-obvious constraints and design decisions, logging is targeted rather than noisy, and defensive checks are mostly at genuine boundaries. The main remaining debt is the isDemosError / isDemosErrorLike duplication in tool-wrapper.ts — two type guards checking the same shape in the same file, with one unused by that file's own logic.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: One confirmed duplicate type guard issue (open since prior review). The safeReadBody dynamic import issue appears resolved — safeParse is statically imported. Overall the codebase avoids the major AI-debt patterns. Score reflects clean code with one narrow duplication issue.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The toolkit migration is well-advanced but incomplete in a concrete, production-impacting way: both primary CLI runners still import the synchronous legacy write-rate-limit instead of the toolkit's async/StateStore version. The deprecated guard exports in index.ts have a @deprecated marker but no concrete removal plan. This creates a genuine dual-implementation state where the same concern (rate limiting) has two implementations with different semantics operating in parallel.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: Two confirmed open issues both still exist: CLI runners use legacy write-rate-limit, and deprecated exports remain without removal timeline. The adapter-specs.ts misplacement is a secondary concern. Score reflects active production code still coupled to the deprecated implementation — worse than a simple stale shim because both systems are live.

package_organization.dimension_character: The toolkit (src/toolkit/) is well-organized with clear purpose-driven subdirectories. The primary organizational problem is src/lib/ — 42 flat files spanning 8+ distinct concern domains with no grouping beyond the existing sources/ subdirectory. A developer navigating from the root cannot predict where to find auth vs. scoring vs. LLM provider vs. TLSN without scanning all 42 files.
package_organization.score_rationale: src/toolkit/ is exemplary. src/lib/ is a known, confirmed overloaded flat directory (4x threshold). The adapter-specs.ts misplacement is a secondary issue. These are both confirmed open issues. Score reflects strong toolkit organization offset by a substantial src/lib/ navigability problem.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The toolkit is well-designed for initialization isolation: all session-specific state is per-instance, SDK imports are lazy, and module-level constants are either frozen literals or lazily-initialized functions. The main residual item is _isContainer in connect.ts — a module-level singleton that is acceptable (container detection is invariant) but technically a boot-order dependency if connect() is called before the process environment is fully initialized.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The previously flagged DEFAULT_STATE_DIR issue appears resolved — homedir() is now called inside a function, not at import time. The _isContainer singleton is intentional with a clear comment. The catalogCache is a legitimate process-level cache. Score reflects good isolation with one minor acceptable singleton.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The toolkit's contracts are largely honest: every tool returns ToolResult<T>, connect's throw behavior is documented, and helper functions match their names. The main gap is getWriteRateRemaining() reading state without pruning or locking — callers may receive a count that differs from what checkAndRecordWrite would compute with a fresh lock acquisition, creating a subtle contract inaccuracy for 'remaining capacity' queries.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Type annotations match return paths throughout. connect()'s throw semantics are explicitly documented. One contract inaccuracy in getWriteRateRemaining (reads unclean state) and the deprecated/active dual implementations keep the score from the 85+ range.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Control flow throughout the toolkit is clear and correct. The retry patterns (verify), parallel guards (publish), and D402 protocol flow (pay) are well-structured. No dead code, always-true conditions, or async-without-await patterns found in the current code. The main clarity concern is subtle: verify.ts's exhaustion path returns ok({confirmed:false}) which a caller must distinguish from the success path ok({confirmed:true}) — both are 'ok' results.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: No logic correctness errors found. The code reads clearly with appropriate comments on non-obvious patterns. Minor clarity issues in verify's exhaustion path and pay's redirect loop prevent the 90+ range. Score reflects clean, intention-revealing control flow.

type_safety.dimension_character: The toolkit's public API is well-typed with bidirectional schema-interface sync assertions. The internal implementation has unavoidable any/unknown zones where the SDK returns opaque shapes — the single-cast-at-factory pattern in sdk-bridge.ts is a reasonable mitigation. The main improvable area is discover-sources.ts catalog parsing, which uses 8 inline casts on untyped entry fields instead of a typed catalog schema.
type_safety.score_rationale: Public API types are rigorous. The schema-interface compile-time assertions are exemplary. Score is held back by catalog entry parsing (8 cast expressions, no Zod schema) and the general SDK casting density in sdk-bridge.ts. These are partial not critical — the casts are isolated and the boundary is documented.

issues.summary: reply() validates input outside withToolWrapper — latencyMs=0 and session.touch() not called on validation failure
issues.suggestion: Move validateInput call inside the withToolWrapper callback (same pattern as publish, attest, etc.), then delegate to publish(). Alternatively, inline reply's validation into publish() which already handles it.
evidence: Lines 62-75: validateInput called before withToolWrapper, err returned with { path: 'local', latencyMs: 0 } hardcoded
Other tools (attest, tip, scan, verify, pay) all call validateInput inside the wrapper fn argument, producing accurate latency
session.touch() is inside withToolWrapper start — skipped when validation fails in reply()

issues.summary: ScanOptions.filters is accepted and typed but never read by scan()

Developer calling scan({ filters: { ... } }) receives no feedback that filters is ignored

issues.summary: apiBaseUrl passed to createSdkBridge is not SSRF-validated — relative-path enforcement cannot protect against a malicious base URL

connect.ts: apiBaseUrl comes from opts.supercolonyApi ?? DEFAULT_SUPERCOLONY_API — user-supplied value, validated only as a URL() by ConnectOptionsSchema (z.string().url()) but NOT validated against SSRF blocklist
attest/pay/publish all call validateUrl on user-supplied URLs; connect() does not call validateUrl on supercolonyApi

evidence: Line 8: imports isDemosError from types.ts
Lines 44-46: catch block uses isDemosError(e) for the check
Lines 58-66: exports isDemosErrorLike() with identical body (code + message + retryable check)

issues.summary: _isContainer module-level singleton in connect.ts reads FS at first call — fragile in test environments
issues.suggestion: Either leave as-is with a test reset helper (export function resetContainerCache(): void { _isContainer = null; }) for test isolation, or move the cache inside a WeakRef/closure pattern. Given the intentional comment explaining the design, adding the test reset export is the minimal fix.
evidence: Line 263: let _isContainer: boolean | null = null — module-level mutable state
Lines 265-291: detectContainer() reads /.dockerenv and /proc/1/cgroup on first call, caches in _isContainer
In test environments, _isContainer persists across test runs if the module is not re-imported — tests that mock FS may see stale cached value

issues.summary: getWriteRateRemaining() reads unpruned state without a lock — may return different count than checkAndRecordWrite would compute
issues.suggestion: Add a comment documenting that getWriteRateRemaining() is approximate and may lag behind the locked state. Alternatively, use checkAndAppend with a no-op check and no entry to get a pruned count (or add a readState helper that acquires the lock).
evidence: Lines 74-91: getWriteRateRemaining() calls loadState() then filters by dayAgo/hourAgo
checkAndAppend() acquires a lock before reading and pruning state.entries
getWriteRateRemaining does not acquire a lock — reads potentially stale state that includes expired entries
A caller querying 'how many writes remain?' may get answer X, then checkAndRecordWrite fails because the locked view prunes to a different count

issues.summary: verify() returns ok({confirmed:false}) after all retries exhaust without error — callers may not distinguish from a definitive 'not confirmed' vs 'gave up checking'
issues.suggestion: Add a comment explaining that ok({confirmed:false}) means 'not found in feed within the retry window' while err(CONFIRM_TIMEOUT) means 'API was unreachable'. The current comment at line 74 ('only checks last 50 posts') partially explains this. Extend it to cover the retry exhaustion case.
evidence: Lines 62-64: if (lastError) returns CONFIRM_TIMEOUT error
Lines 64: return ok<VerifyResult>({ confirmed: false }, ...) — returned when all retries complete without error but tx not found

A tx genuinely not in the feed (e.g., older than 50 posts) and a tx that timed out both produce ok({confirmed:false}) — but the timeout path produces err(), so non-found is ok. This is actually correct behavior — the ambiguity is documented in a comment.

issues.summary: discover-sources.ts parses catalog entries with 8 inline Record<string,unknown> casts — no typed interface or Zod schema for catalog structure
issues.suggestion: Define a CatalogEntrySchema with Zod (or a plain interface) matching the catalog.json structure. Use it in loadCatalog() to replace the 8 inline casts with a single safeParse call. This would also catch catalog corruption at load time rather than silently producing empty strings.
evidence: Lines 71-82: entry typed as Record<string, unknown> with casts on every field: (entry.id as string), (entry.name as string), (entry.domain as string), etc.
No CatalogEntry interface or Zod schema exists for the catalog JSON structure
Contrast with D402RequirementSchema in schemas.ts — the same team applied Zod to external API responses but not to the internal catalog file

issues.summary: fetchWithValidatedRedirects in pay.ts has no explicit return type annotation
issues.suggestion: Add ': Promise<Response>' to the function signature.
evidence: Lines 250-299: async function fetchWithValidatedRedirects(session, url, init, pinnedIp?) — return type not annotated
TypeScript infers Promise<Response> correctly but the function signature is part of a complex protocol flow
All other exported functions in the toolkit have explicit return types

add.description: connect() throws DemosError rather than returning ToolResult — this is intentional and documented in the JSDoc. It manages session lifecycle, not tool operations. Not an API coherence defect.

add.header: Two dead option fields break API honesty
add.description: ScanOptions.filters and AttestOptions.claimType are accepted by the public API but have no effect at runtime. This creates a false contract where callers cannot tell their input was ignored.

add.header: Bridge relative-path enforcement closes absolute-URL SSRF
add.description: bridge.apiCall rejects absolute URLs (http:// / https://) at the bridge level. This means scan/verify/react/tip cannot be made to call attacker-controlled URLs via the apiCall path. The remaining risk is the apiBaseUrl itself, which is a configuration parameter not a runtime input.

add.header: supercolonyApi base URL not SSRF-validated at connect
add.description: opts.supercolonyApi is validated as a well-formed URL by Zod but not checked against the SSRF IP blocklist. This is consistent with treating it as trusted configuration, but the asymmetry with attest/pay URL validation is worth documenting.
safeParse is now statically imported at module level in pay.ts (line 15). The previously flagged dynamic import pattern no longer exists.

add.header: CLI runners actively use legacy rate limiter
add.description: This is not a dormant shim — both session-runner.ts and event-runner.ts import from the deprecated src/lib/write-rate-limit.ts. The toolkit write-rate-limit exists but is only used by the toolkit's own publish() tool, not by the CLI orchestrators.
The toolkit has clear purpose-driven subdirectories (tools/, guards/) with a clean barrel export at index.ts. This is the organizational model the rest of the codebase should follow.
state-store.ts uses getDefaultStateDir() as a function called at constructor time, not a module-level constant computed at import time. The previously flagged issue is resolved.
All SDK module imports (DemosTransactions, D402Client, Demos) are lazy-imported inside async functions, preventing module-level side effects from crypto initialization and global state mutation.

add.header: ToolResult return type is consistent across all tools
add.description: Every public tool (publish, reply, react, tip, scan, verify, attest, pay, discoverSources) returns Promise<ToolResult<T>> with correct type parameters. Error paths use err() which correctly types as ToolResult<T>. No return type annotation lies found.

add.header: Parallel guard execution in publish is correct
add.description: publish() runs checkAndRecordWrite and checkAndRecordDedup in parallel via Promise.all for the check phase, then again for the record phase. The check phase passes false (no mutation) and record phase passes true — correct atomic semantics.

add.header: Bidirectional schema-interface sync assertions are exemplary
add.description: schemas.ts has compile-time forward + reverse assertions for all 10 option types. This eliminates schema/interface drift without runtime cost. No other part of the codebase does this — it's a strong positive signal for the toolkit's type discipline.

add.header: Catalog entry parsing lacks typed schema
add.description: discover-sources.ts parses catalog.json with 8 inline casts. A CatalogEntrySchema was not added when Zod was adopted — this is the main remaining type safety gap in the toolkit.
